item.title: US eases Russia oil sanctions as Iran war pushes up energy prices
item.description: Washington says a 30-day waiver is only for sanctioned Russian oil already loaded on vessels at sea.

item.title: Starmer and Martin agree on infrastructure projects at UK-Ireland summit
item.description: The second summit follows the announcement of new Irish investment of more than £900m into the UK.

item.title: Israel-Iran Conflict: Iran War Live Updates: Hegseth Vows to Reopen Key Strait as U.S. Measures Fail to Calm Oil Fears
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said that the U.S. military would stop Iran from choking off the Strait of Hormuz but did not say how. Oil prices remained high even after the Trump administration eased restrictions on Russian shipments.

item.title: Pink Floyd Guitar Is Sold for a Record $14.55 Million
item.description: The black Fender Stratocaster, played by David Gilmour on six of the band’s albums including “The Dark Side of the Moon,” broke the record for the most expensive guitar sold at auction.

item.title: Cuba Acknowledges Talks with Trump Officials For the First Time
item.description: Facing a massive energy crisis and increasing protests on the streets, the Cuban government acknowledged that it had entered into negotiations with the Trump administration.

item.title: France returns sacred 'talking drum' looted during colonial rule to Ivory Coast
item.description: The move forms part of a broader process of returning cultural artefacts to African countries that started in 2017.‎

item.title: Oil and cargo ships are growing targets in war with Iran.
UK urges allies to keep up pressure on Russia as US eases sanctions
item.description: Ministers say the UK will not be following the US by loosening sanctions on some Russian oil.

item.title: US economy ended 2025 on weaker footing than previously thought
item.description: Revised figures show GDP rose at a 0.7% rate in fourth quarter, down from earlier reading of 1.4%

item.title: Hegseth says Iran leader wounded as US vows to intensify strikes
item.description: EU hits back at Trump administration’s decision to lift sanctions on Russian oil

item.title: Dubai’s financial district rattled by Iranian drone attacks
item.description: Towers in Dubai International Financial Centre suffer damage but most drones and missiles are intercepted

item.title: Bored of peace
item.description: The idea of making difficult trade-offs is alien to Trump

item.title: How Iran’s fightback surprised the US
item.description: Tehran has done ‘more with less’ as it hit major targets around the Gulf, but its firing has begun to wane

item.title: Here’s the latest.
Games with loot boxes to get minimum 16 age rating across Europe
item.description: The new changes by the Pan-European Game Information age-ratings body (PEGI) will start from June.

item.title: Petrol retailers deny price gouging accusations
item.description: Ed Miliband says the competition watchdog is primed to intervene if firms use the oil price shock to "rip off" customers.

item.title: France and Italy open talks with Iran in hope of securing safe Hormuz passage
item.description: Europeans look for ways to restart energy shipments as Iran’s new supreme leader vows to keep strait shut

item.title: UK and Ireland pledge joint exercises to protect cables from Russian spy ships
item.description: Update to defence and security pact will see countries ‘tackle hostile threats and crack down on the shadow fleet’

item.title: Four crew members killed after US refuelling plane crashes in Iraq
item.description: The US Central Command says rescue efforts continue after a refuelling aircraft went down over western Iraq.

item.title: To Secure BTS Concert Tickets, K-Pop Fans Crowd Internet Cafes in South Korea
item.description: The K-pop supergroup’s upcoming reunion concert prompted a rush for the cafes, which offer solid connections and a sense of community.

item.title: Farage regrets taking control of 'bankrupt' council
item.description: The Reform UK leader says he wishes the party "hadn't bothered" to take control in Worcestershire.

item.title: Is Paris ready for the super-rich?
item.description: The upcoming launch of the city’s first elite branded residence is riding on a wave of super-prime sales. But amid calls for new wealth taxes, tensions are high

item.title: Cancelling the UK national debt, £1.7mn at a time
item.description: Your Debt Management Office needs you

item.title: 'No-one will hire women' - India's top court rejects menstrual leave petition
item.description: The subject has polarised India for long, though some states and private companies offer menstrual leaves.

item.title: Poland’s Eurosceptic president vetoes EU funding for defence
item.description: Karol Nawrocki opposes Safe loans, claiming they would give Brussels a say in national security matters

item.title: UK economy flatlines in January as people cut back on eating out
item.description: Analysts had been expecting 0.2% growth for the UK economy at the beginning of the year.

item.title: Turkey Says NATO Defenses Shot Down a Third Iranian Missile
item.description: Turkey did not say where the missile was intercepted. But residents near Incirlik Air Base, which hosts U.S. troops, reported hearing sirens and a loud boom.

item.title: Lessons from an unwise banking photo shoot
item.description: An interview with four young men in finance dressed in Armani jackets and Hermès ties exposes several tensions

item.title: Phones ‘Ringing Off the Hook’ for Ukraine Defense Firms as Mideast Seeks Help
item.description: Ukraine wants to leverage its defense expertise into security partnerships and to reap potentially vast profits for its arms industry.

item.title: The best memento of 2026 is a restaurant matchbox
item.description: These tiny designs are almost artworks in themselves

item.title: Bodies of 84 Iranian sailors killed in US torpedo strike to be repatriated
item.description: The seamen died when Iranian warship the Iris Dena was sunk on 4 March by a US submarine near Sri Lanka.

item.title: China Wants Its Ethnic Minorities to Blend In. Now It’s the Law.
item.description: Under a new “ethnic unity” law, Mandarin Chinese must now be the language of teaching. Parents must guide their children to love the Communist Party. Neighborhoods should be mixed.

item.title: What Does It Mean to Have Air Superiority Over Iran?
item.description: The American and Israeli air forces have a dominant advantage in the skies, but Iran can still muster some resistance.

item.title: UK to speed up nuclear power projects by weakening wildlife protection
item.description: Government will adopt proposals from Fingleton review, to the dismay of ecologists

item.title: Drone Strike Has Cyprus, and Europe, on Edge
item.description: Allies have rushed to defend the Mediterranean nation, where the drone hit a British base. Some Cypriots wonder why the bases are still there.

item.title: Is Jonah Freud’s Reference Point the sexiest library in the world?
item.description: Meet the men still championing print

item.title: High student fees plus poor funding model will satisfy nobody
item.description: With Labour relying on working-age voters, cost of education is now a big political problem for government

item.title: Hong Kong plans to ‘name and shame’ lawyers and auditors for sloppy IPO work
item.description: Proposal comes as regulators grapple with influx of Chinese companies looking to raise funds in the city

item.title: Minister in Belgium amid concern over migrant boats
item.description: The visit comes as people smugglers target the Belgian coast, away from main launch sites in France.

item.title: Post Office scandal 'has taken 21 years of my life'
item.description: Seema Misra calls for accountability as a report by MPs raises concerns about ongoing delays.

item.title: Where Israeli Strikes Are Hitting Beirut
item.description: Our Beirut bureau chief, Christina Goldbaum, shows how Israeli airstrikes have affected Lebanon and its capital. Hundreds of thousands of people have fled areas around Beirut and in a huge swath of southern Lebanon after Israel issued evacuation warnings in its conflict with Hezbollah.

item.title: ‘A Lot of Life Years Lost’: How NAFTA Shortened American Life Spans
item.description: A study tracks how the North American Free Trade Agreement and trade competition with Mexico led to earlier deaths for American factory workers.

item.title: UK economy unexpectedly failed to grow in January
item.description: Figure comes as surging energy prices pose new threat

item.title: Draper beaten after controversial hindrance call
item.description: Jack Draper is penalised with a controversial hindrance call as Daniil Medvedev ends the Briton's Indian Wells title defence in the quarter-finals.

item.title: Australian businessman found guilty of working for suspected Chinese spies
item.description: Csergo argued his reports did not contain any secrets but only information available on the internet.

item.title: Gulf oil shock deepens crisis for Asia’s petrochemicals industry
item.description: Japan and South Korea exposed to shortages of key component for plastics

item.title: The US consumer is OK
item.description: But if oil stays expensive . . .

item.title: EU chief diplomat warns of ‘chaos’ and open-ended Middle East war
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Brussels prepares to launch new corporate regime

item.title: South Korea police raid transport ministry over Jeju Air crash
item.description: More inquiries into the incident have been launched, as investigators discover more body parts and victims' belongings.

item.title: The US may move some of its anti-missile system - and it's sparking unease in South Korea
item.description: Reports say the US may be moving parts of its Thaad system to the Middle East, despite Seoul's opposition.

item.title: A Visit to a Temple at the Heart of the Thailand-Cambodia Conflict
item.description: A rare visit to a Khmer temple on Thailand and Cambodia’s border showed how deadly clashes between the two countries have scarred a heritage site.

item.title: How the War in Iran Could Help China and Change Asia
item.description: American officials have said for years that they would prioritize the Indo-Pacific. Now they’re moving warships, missiles and air defenses out for a war in the Middle East.

item.title: KPop Demon Hunters to return as Netflix announces sequel
item.description: The sequel brings back the co-directors of the first film, which was a smash-hit for the streaming service.

item.title: Churchill's granddaughter happy with his picture being replaced on £5 note
item.description: Emma Soames rejected Nigel Farage's statement that the currency change was "wokery".

item.title: 'There's no hiding place on a ship': The sailors stranded near Iran
item.description: Drones, cruise missiles and fighter jets have become a common sight for many sailors stranded on ships in the Gulf.

item.title: MPs 'deeply troubled' by BBC World Service funding uncertainty
item.description: The current funding agreement between the BBC and the government expires at the end of this month.

item.title: Qantas agrees to pay $74m over Covid-19 travel voucher refunds
item.description: The case relates to cancelled flights during the pandemic, for which customers were given credits instead of cash.

item.title: French vote tests polarised electorate with right hoping to win control of Paris
item.description: The highest-profile contest is for the mayorship of Paris - which has been under left-wing control for 25 years.

item.title: Mission accomplished? The 2003 boast that haunts today's Iran conflict
item.description: The echoes between the conflicts are certainly there but there are also profound differences.

item.title: Why Donald Trump Doesn’t Want to Talk About the War in Iran
item.description: “We won,” the President who’s treating the conflict with Iran like a video game says, but “we’re not finished yet.”

item.title: Trump Tempers María Corina Machado’s Political Ambitions in Venezuela
item.description: President Trump is tempering the political ambitions of María Corina Machado, a Nobel laureate, as he deepens ties with her foes in Venezuela.

item.title: Israel pounds Beirut suburbs and south Lebanon after Hezbollah rocket barrage
item.description: Some Israeli strikes also hit other parts of the Lebanese capital, with 12 people reportedly killed along the seafront.

item.title: 'Follow the smoke': BBC spends day with emergency teams as Israel strikes south Lebanon
item.description: The BBC visited the city of Nabatieh, witnessing the pace and scale of Israel's bombing as its war with Hezbollah continues to escalate.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader vows to block Strait of Hormuz in first statement
item.description: A message attributed to Mojtaba Khamenei also says Iran will continue targeting US bases in the region.

item.title: PM says Mandelson appointment was mistake as No 10 denies cover-up
item.description: Documents show Sir Keir Starmer was warned the peer's relationship with Jeffrey Epstein posed a "reputational risk".

item.title: Emirati minister tells BBC Iran must end strikes on Gulf
item.description: The UAE says more than 1,800 drones and missiles have been projected at the country since the war began.

item.title: UK troops at Iraq base shot down Iranian drones, Healey says
item.description: British forces based in Iraq shot down two drones overnight, Defence Secretary John Healey has confirmed.

item.title: Watchdog says he 'can't trust ministers' with Sturgeon inquiry files
item.description: The information commissioner has threatened the government with more legal action over the release of files.

item.title: Israel Drops Case Against Soldiers Accused of Abusing Palestinian Detainee
item.description: After a prisoner arrived at a hospital with broken ribs and a torn rectum, Israelis were once again at odds over the issue of mistreatment and impunity.

item.title: I can survive no-confidence vote - council leader
item.description: Reform UK's George Finch says he is confident he will stay in charge on Warwickshire County Council.

item.title: Globetrotting Constantine back in charge of Rwanda
item.description: Rwanda reappoint globetrotting Englishman Stephen Constantine as head coach of their men's national team on a two-year contract.

item.title: Tracking the wave of ship attacks that has choked off Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Six ships have been reportedly attacked in the Gulf in less than 48 hours, bringing the total vessels attacked during the war to 18.

item.title: France's ghost car scandal that allowed one million illegal vehicles onto the roads
item.description: Fake dealerships were manipulating the state vehicle licensing agency's official records, France's auditor has found.

item.title: British man charged in Dubai for alleged filming of Iranian missiles
item.description: Foreign Office officials are understood to be supporting his family.

item.title: Jo Malone sued for using her own name in collaboration with Zara
item.description: The perfumier sold the rights to her name in 1999 but has previously said she regretted the move.

item.title: Parents accused of hiding daughter's HIV infection and neglect
item.description: The child did not see a doctor until she was six - and had not had any vaccinations, Italian media say.

item.title: Senegal approves tougher anti-gay law as rights groups raise concerns
item.description: "Violence and fear" will spike and "equality and non-discrimination" will be trampled on, warn critics.

item.title: Swinney seeks UK meeting over US military use of Prestwick Airport
item.description: Three American military refuelling aircraft that previously stopped at the airport are believed to be supporting US-Israeli airstrikes.

item.title: Rapper-politician Balendra Shah's party wins Nepal election
item.description: The Rastriya Swatatantra Party has won a large majority in an election dominated by issues including corruption.

item.title: A popular German Green wins a surprise victory
item.description: Cem Özdemir becomes the first Turkish-German to run a state

item.title: In Paris’s mayoral race, it’s drivers against cyclists
item.description: A decade of greening leaves the capital less congested but more divided

item.title: As war rages, Turkey‘s strongman puts the opposition on trial
item.description: Chaos in the Middle East makes President Erdogan indispensable to the West

item.title: Kenya’s ailing sugar sector is a test case for reform
item.description: Low productivity and political corruption bedevil the whole economy

item.title: What data reveal about the war’s progress
item.description: We analyse 1,615 distinct attacks, and more besides

item.title: Iran’s praetorian guard may emerge from the war diminished but undefeated
item.description: The regime’s enforcers will struggle to rebuild their capabilities and their deterrence

item.title: Who is Iran’s new leader?
item.description: No one knows if he will last

item.title: Islamists woo Bangladesh with everything but Islam
item.description: They are a hit on campus, too

item.title: Taiwan’s bid to export drones free of Chinese parts is taking off
item.description: The island’s unmanned aerial vehicles are already in use on Ukraine’s front line

item.title: Nepal’s new prime minister is a 35-year-old former rapper
item.description: Voters have given Balendra Shah an unprecedented mandate and enormous power

item.title: Seoul’s housing market is a huge political and economic headache
item.description: Lee Jae Myung is staking his presidency on fixing it

item.title: Brazilian cinema is having its moment
item.description: Thank growing competition and a more internationally minded Academy awards

item.title: China’s nationalist spy thriller has few girls and lots of government
item.description: “Main melody” films are in a rut

item.title: Hong Kong’s property market has turned
item.description: That’s not good news for everyone

item.title: Some of China’s officials are becoming social-media stars
item.description: It may help them get promotions

item.title: Haiti needs order first, then elections
item.description: Voters must be able to turn out without risking death

item.title: How to teach Donald Trump a Latin lesson
item.description: By alienating Hispanics, he has given Democrats an open goal

item.title: China’s hereditary elite is taking shape
item.description: The Communist Party is afraid to tax inherited wealth

item.title: Death toll from Ethiopia floods and landslides rises to more than 100
item.description: Forecasters say storms are becoming more intense in the region, partly due to global warming.

item.title: How Iran war laid bare the world's reliance on Gulf oil and gas
item.description: Countries around the world are feeling the impact of the conflict and the resulting energy price shock.

item.title: Cartoonist freed after 15 years in prison without charge in Eritrea
item.description: Biniam Solomon was known for his witty and critical cartoons before the free press was closed.

item.title: Nineteen jailed over deadly Moscow concert attack
item.description: Gunmen opened fire at Crocus City Concert hall near Moscow in 2024, killing 149 and injuring more than 500.

item.title: 'What if we're left with ruins?': Doubts creep in for Iranians who supported war
item.description: Teachers, engineers and shopkeepers tell the BBC they fear Iran falling into chaos.

item.title: One house, two faiths, one fasting season
item.description: Ramadan and Lent haven't overlapped since 1993 and couples like Olanrewaju and Kaosara in Nigeria are observing them together.

item.title: Iran’s Frantic Attempt to Save Its Ships Before Torpedo Attack
item.description: The Iranian Navy sought refuge in Sri Lanka and India. While India obliged, Sri Lanka stalled over fears it would threaten its neutrality.

item.title: Questions mount for Hegseth over possible US involvement in strike on Iranian school
item.description: Democrats have written to ask the defence secretary whether the US was responsible, but he says the issue is being investigated.

item.title: Polanski stood by breast enlargement hypnosis claim
item.description: A newly unearthed interview from 2013 casts doubt on the Green Party leader's claim to have apologised.

item.title: Ground Down by War, Hezbollah’s Loyal Base Shows Cracks
item.description: “We just want to be back in our homes,” said a Lebanese man who, like many others in the latest round of fighting, has to flee.

item.title: An attack on the world economy
item.description: Whatever happens in the Strait of Hormuz, energy markets have been changed for ever

item.title: There are no good options for Iran’s nuclear programme
item.description: If America cannot eliminate the threat, what should it do?

item.title: What role has cyber warfare played in Iran?
item.description: Militaries are often cagey about their cyber activities. But the US has hinted at the role it has played.

item.title: I've lost friends since joining Reform, says Offord
item.description: The ex-Conservative peer says he has made a "sacrifice" to join Nigel Farage's party as its leader in Scotland.

item.title: 'Fingers on the trigger': Deadly warnings for Iranians being urged to take action
item.description: Iran's police chief has warned that his forces would treat anyone taking to the streets "at the enemy's request" as an enemy.

item.title: War in Ukraine spills into Hungarian election campaign
item.description: Hungary is going to the polls soon and Prime Minister Viktor Orbán has one public enemy in his sights - Ukraine's president.

item.title: How Russia’s Scorched-Earth Attacks Put Ukraine’s Power Grid Near Collapse
item.description: Strikes on Ukrainian energy systems have tripled this winter. Continuous repairs and Western aid staved off a total breakdown.

item.title: Chinese national arrested over attempt to smuggle 2,000 queen ants from Kenya
item.description: The suspect had packed some ants in test tubes while others were concealed in tissue paper rolls, prosecutor says.

item.title: China approves 'ethnic unity' law requiring minorities to learn Mandarin
item.description: The law states that children should be taught Mandarin before kindergarten, until the end of high school.

item.title: What did we learn from China's biggest political meeting?
item.description: BBC correspondents give us their biggest takeaways from China's National People's Congress.

item.title: 'The situation is dire': War on Iran squeezes India's cooking-gas supplies
item.description: The shockwaves of the US-Israeli strikes on Iran are being felt in India's kitchens.

item.title: Video shows moment point-blank gunshot misses Indian politician
item.description: Senior Kashmir leader Farooq Abdullah escaped unhurt after the incident and the suspect is in custody.

item.title: Explosions reported on two foreign vessels in the Gulf, authorities say
item.description: Pressure has intensified on the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's most important shipping lanes, since Israel and the US attacked Iran last month.

item.title: Noma head chef resigns from restaurant amid abuse allegations
item.description: René Redzepi had previously apologised after ex-employees of the Danish restaurant accused him of creating a toxic work environment.

item.title: Bodies of two Chinese backpackers found in Australian floodwaters
item.description: The pair had been reported missing on Tuesday after failing to reach their destination.

item.title: The 24 hours that damaged the Premier League's best-in-the-world reputation
item.description: Phil McNulty examines the state of the Premier League after none of its six Champions League entrants won this week.

item.title: Epstein used modelling agent to recruit girls, Brazilian women tell BBC
item.description: Modelling agent used businesses to recruit girls and arrange US visas to visit Jeffrey Epstein, Brazilian women tell BBC.

item.title: Why Namibia's green energy dream could be a red flag for penguins
item.description: A near pristine desert and coastal wilderness in Namibia could soon host a huge hydrogen production facility.

item.title: Student loans inquiry to look at whether system is 'unfair to graduates'
item.description: The Treasury Committee will look at whether "the goalposts [have] been moved in a way which is unfair".

item.title: Union cuts Labour funding by 40% over bin strike
item.description: The union votes in favour of an "unprecedented" £580,000 cut, due to anger over ongoing action.

item.title: Viktor Orban’s illiberal intellectual patronage system
item.description: The Budapest Megaphone blares populism to the world

item.title: PM was warned of 'reputational risk' over Mandelson's Epstein links
item.description: Documents also suggest the peer explored the possibility of a £500,000 severance payment after being sacked as US ambassador.

item.title: Could special forces truly obliterate Iran’s nuclear programme?
item.description: Swiping the country’s uranium would require one of the largest raids in military history

item.title: At last, Haiti has some hope
item.description: Aggressive policing is weakening gangsters’ control

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: The real meaning of the Iran war for Africa
item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, writes about the Middle East crisis

item.title: EU and UK demand Israel stop surge in West Bank settler violence since Iran war
item.description: Six Palestinians have been killed during attacks by settlers in the West Bank since the start of Israel's war, the UN says.

item.title: Fuel tax hike plan to be kept under review over Iran, says PM
item.description: Fuel duty on petrol and diesel is due to rise from September, when a 5p cut is phased out.

item.title: Zelensky sends drone teams to Middle East, touting Ukraine's expertise
item.description: The Ukrainian president said it was a "good feeling" when allies asked for help countering drones.

item.title: Ukraine says it has hit Russian 'missile component' plant
item.description: Russia says the attack hit civilians and could not have been carried out without British help.

item.title: The Mugabe family after losing power - arrests, accusations and arguments
item.description: Bellarmine Mugabe will negotiate a plea deal after being accused of attempted murder and other charges in South Africa.

item.title: Inside the Australian hotel where Iranian footballers escaped to claim asylum
item.description: Activists played a key role in making contact with the women as they tried to dodge their minders.

item.title: Jeffrey Epstein had two key aides - why do they still control his money and secrets?
item.description: Richard Kahn and Darren Indyke administer Epstein’s estate - court filings allege complicity in his crimes.

item.title: Hereditary peers to be removed from Lords as bill passes
item.description: The bill abolishes the 92 seats reserved for peers who inherit their titles through their families.

item.title: Can America clear the Strait of Hormuz of Iran’s drones and mines?
item.description: Escorting convoys of oil tanker with warships may give Iran juicy American targets

item.title: Acclaimed Peruvian writer Alfredo Bryce Echenique dies aged 87
item.description: The author is best known for his novel A World for Julius, which chronicles the frivolous lives of the elite.

item.title: Why air strikes on Tehran oil facilities are causing black rain
item.description: Air strikes have damaged at least four oil facilities since US-Israeli attacks on Iran began last month.

item.title: Steve Rosenberg: Russia seeks diplomatic and economic gains from Iran war
item.description: President Putin pits himself as a potential mediator but that's not an easy sell, writes the BBC's Russia editor.

item.title: Ukraine’s housing market is increasingly peculiar
item.description: With blood in the streets, many are buying property

item.title: America’s war aims may be diverging from Israel’s
item.description: For Binyamin Netanyahu they are, as ever, a question of politics
